_doc: Route verification corpus (PLAN-7 Phase 0 / F-41). Verified historical observations about how ships actually sailed, checked against the BAKED polylines in data/routes.json by research/tools/route-verify.mjs. Schema + rules in _schema.md. A prescribed route is not an observed route; `kind` keeps them apart. Tolerances must match the source's precision -- tightening one beyond what the source supports is fabricated precision, which is the main way this file could go wrong.
_seed: SEED SET, 2026-07-21. Every entry below derives from research/port-flow-candidates-waystations-2026-07-20.md -- the T14 waystations sweep, gathered by three basin agents and adversarially self-checked, then cross-checked against the repo's lanes at synthesis. That makes it real in-repo provenance rather than invention, and it lets the harness be tested before R-11 lands. R-11 replaces this with a corpus built from external sources (CLIWOC, sailing directions, company records).
_note_on_circularity: Six of these constraints are implemented in the baker as `via` chains, so it may look circular to test them. It is not. The harness measures the GEOMETRY of the baked polyline -- whether the track actually passes within tolerance of Table Bay -- not whether a `via` field is set. The Acapulco->Guam->Manila longitude-reframing bug produced a route with a correct `via` and a track that jumped a circumference; that class of defect is exactly what these entries catch. The forbidden-corridor entries are not implied by `via` at all: a route can carry via:anjer and still clip Malacca.

entries.notes: The sweep's headline correction: the Europe-China route ran through the Sunda Strait, not Malacca. Malacca was a TERMINUS, not a waystop on the China run. The forbidden-corridor half is an independent test -- carrying via:anjer does not by itself keep a track out of the Malacca box. Tolerance 450 km reflects that the sources place the call at the strait, not at a berth.

The refreshment call that made Table Bay exist as a settlement. Gated to the 1652 founding: before it, ships rounded the Cape without stopping, which is why the era window starts there and not at 1550.

HOMEWARD and BRITISH. The sweep's first structural finding is that direction and nationality both matter and a nationality-blind 'universal waystop' tag would overstate every one of these. The negative case is a separate entry.

The negative of the entry above, and the more valuable test: it fails if the harness or the baker ever flattens the nationality split into a universal call. St Helena was English/British; Dutch and Swedish homeward Indiamen did not use it.
VOC standing orders ran direct to Table Bay -- which is the reason Table Bay exists. A Dutch outbound Indiaman calling at Madeira would be a real fidelity error, not a cosmetic one.

The westbound galleon watered at Guam. The eastbound return did not -- it stood far north into the westerlies before running down the American coast, which is the asymmetry entry below. This is also the lane whose per-hop longitude re-framing bug once made the track jump a whole circumference, so the geometric check here is load-bearing.

The defining asymmetry of the Pacific: the westbound run took the trades, the eastbound return stood north for the westerlies. A router producing a symmetric out-and-back here is wrong in a way no amount of geometric polish would hide.

sources: research/port-flow-candidates-waystations-2026-07-20.md (the chains differ: madeira->cape-town->anjer outbound vs anjer->cape-town->st-helena homeward)
entries.notes: Outbound and homeward carried different waystop chains, so the tracks must visibly differ. 200 km is deliberately loose -- both legs cross the same oceans and share the Cape; the test is that they are not the same line.
